title: Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism
subtitle: Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a [terrorist attack](#dod:terrorism).
caption:
Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism

Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a terrorist attack.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of people worried about terrorism and the share of deaths from terrorism across various countries, with circle sizes representing the number of attacks. The vertical axis measures the share of people worried about terrorism, ranging from 30% to over 90%, while the horizontal axis plots the share of deaths from terrorism on a logarithmic scale from 0.0001% to 1%. Countries are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. Iraq and Nigeria occupy the upper-right quadrant, showing both high worry levels—around 85% to 90%—and high death shares near 0.1% to 1%, with Iraq’s large circle indicating a high number of attacks. Myanmar and the Maldives also show high worry shares above 90% but lower death shares around 0.01%. In contrast, New Zealand and the Netherlands sit in the lower-left, with worry shares below 35% and death shares under 0.001%. European countries like Germany, the United Kingdom, and Greece cluster in the lower-middle range, with worry shares between 40% and 60% and death shares below 0.001%. Asian countries display wide variation: China reports 60% worry with minimal death share, while Thailand and Kuwait show moderate worry (45–65%) and higher death shares near 0.01% to 0.1%. African nations such as Zimbabwe, Kenya, and Egypt fall in the middle-to-upper worry range (70–85%) with death shares spanning 0.001% to 0.1%. The data shows no consistent linear relationship between worry and death shares; some countries with low death shares report high worry, while others with higher death shares report moderate or low worry. The most visible pattern is the wide dispersion of points across the chart, with no clear clustering along a diagonal trend line.
